title: CDMGNotation

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_2 -- "CDMGNotation".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_2
% title: CDMGNotation
\def\rowref{def\_3\_2}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_2}

\begin{Not}[CDMGNotation]
    \label{not-cdmg}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG.
    We will write:
    \begin{enumerate}
        %\item $G(V|\doit(J))$ to represent the graph $(J,V,E,L)$, where $E$ and $L$ are kept implicit in this notation.
        \item $v \in G$ to mean $v \in J \cup V$,
        \item $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in E$,
        \item $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_2,v_1) \in E$,
        \item $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in L$,
        \item $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
        \item $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
        \item $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$.
    \end{enumerate}
    The star stands for a placeholder to mean: ``arrowhead or tail''.
\end{Not}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG. We will write:

  1. $v \in G$ to mean $v \in J \cup V$,
  2. $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in E$,
  3. $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_2,v_1) \in E$,
  4. $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in L$,
  5. $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  6. $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  7. $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$.

The star stands for a placeholder to mean: “arrowhead or tail”.

lean_code_with_comments:
instance instMembership : Membership Node (CDMG Node) where
  mem G v := v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V

def tuh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v1, v2) ∈ G.E

def hut (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v2, v1) ∈ G.E

def huh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v1, v2) ∈ G.L

def suh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2

def hus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2

def sus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2
lean_code_without_comments:
instance instMembership : Membership Node (CDMG Node) where
  mem G v := v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V
def tuh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v1, v2) ∈ G.E
def hut (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v2, v1) ∈ G.E
def huh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v1, v2) ∈ G.L
def suh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2
def hus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2
def sus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2
addition_to_the_LN:
[sus_omits_tail_tail_despite_star_eq_head_or_tail] CDMGs do not admit a tail-tail edge type; consequently the placeholder rule "the star stands for arrowhead or tail" yields only the three enumerated configurations in item 7 (the tail-tail case "$v_1 \tut v_2$" is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight). Any case analysis on "$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$" may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

[huh_visual_symmetry_vs_ordered_pair_in_L] The relation $L$ is required to be symmetric: for all $v_1, v_2$, $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ if and only if $(v_2, v_1) \in L$. Consequently, $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \iff v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$, and the symmetry of the $\huh$-disjunct propagates to items 5–7 accordingly.
design_choices:
## Design choices

**Stay literal with the LN at every call site.** The seven items are translated as the most surface-faithful Lean construct available for each shape: item 1 is a `Membership` instance so that `v ∈ G` parses verbatim with no `G.Mem v` detour, items 2–4 unfold directly to `(v1, v2) ∈ G.E` / `(v2, v1) ∈ G.E` / `(v1, v2) ∈ G.L`, and items 5–7 are literal `∨`-disjunctions mirroring the LN enumeration. Every downstream row that quantifies over "vertices of $G$" or pattern-matches on edge shape can therefore be transcribed without a translation table.

**Def names equal the LaTeX macro names.** Identifiers `tuh`, `hut`, `huh`, `suh`, `hus`, `sus` are the exact LN macros, placed under the `CDMG` namespace so that dot-notation `G.tuh v1 v2` reads left-to-right like $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$. A reader of the lecture notes can grep for the macro and land on its Lean counterpart immediately.

**Plain `def`, not `notation` or `abbrev`.** A unicode `notation` macro was rejected because it would either bake in the graph $G$ (forcing `notation3` with a graph slot) or lose the graph context entirely. `abbrev` was rejected to keep rewriting behaviour stable: downstream proofs (`def_3_3`, `def_3_4`, `def_3_6`, `claim_3_2`) call `unfold CDMG.tuh` only when they need the raw `Finset` membership, whereas an `abbrev` would unfold eagerly and mask intent.

**`hut` is a primitive `def`, not `tuh ∘ swap` or `G.tuh v2 v1`.** The LN's `def_3_3` distinguishes "edges of the form $v_1 \hut v_2$" as a syntactic shape, not an `iff`-rewrite. Giving `hut` its own definition mirrors that surface form, and a direct `(v2, v1) ∈ G.E` body keeps `simp only [CDMG.hut]` landing on `Finset` membership without intermediate `Function.comp` or `Prod.swap` peeling — a price that would be paid at every walk/parent/acyclicity proof otherwise.

**Symmetry of `huh` is delegated to `hL_symm`, not inlined.** The bidirected relation could have been encoded as `(v1, v2) ∈ G.L ∨ (v2, v1) ∈ G.L`, but that would force every `CDMG`-level `hL_symm` invocation to also rewrite through `huh`. Keeping the body as the literal asymmetric pair lookup lets the symmetry surface as a one-line corollary of `hL_symm`, and the same goes for irreflexivity via `hL_irrefl`.

**`sus` has three disjuncts, not four.** Although the star-rule "arrowhead or tail" mechanically suggests `tut ∨ tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh`, CDMGs carry no tail-tail edge type, so `sus` is `tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh` and downstream case analyses may treat these as exhaustive. A refactor warning is attached: if a future `def_3_1` revision adds a `T` field, `sus` and every consumer of its exhaustiveness contract must be revisited.

**Disjunction order matches the LN reading.** In `suh`, `hus`, and `sus`, the disjuncts appear in the same left-to-right order as the tex enumeration, keeping `rcases` patterns and case names aligned with the lecture notes.

**Use `G.J ∪ G.V`, not paired disjoint tests.** Membership only asks whether $v$ lies in either set; the disjointness witness `hJV_disj` is invoked at use sites (e.g. `def_3_10`'s rewriting of $V \setminus W$) rather than threaded through `instMembership`. This also avoids any clash with `Finset.instMembership`, since the two instances differ in their container type and Lean resolves them transparently.
